learning_objective: Students will systematically analyze simulated collision data to link kinetic energy to physical changes, evaluating through peer argumentation whether energy transfer or force is the primary mechanism driving these alterations.
core_ideas: The motion of an object is determined by the sum of the forces acting on it; if the total force on the object is not zero, its motion will change.
A collision can cause the objects involved to change motion and/or change shape.
Energy transfer occurs during a collision.
There is a force(s) between objects when they make contact during a collision.

misconceptions.1-a: A force is needed to keep something moving at constant speed.
misconceptions.1-b: Balanced forces mean the object must be at rest.
misconceptions.2-a: If motion changes, there is a change in force.
misconceptions.3-a: Energy is lost/disappears in an inelastic ('sticky') collision.
misconceptions.3-b: Momentum and energy are the same (or both are always conserved).

quiz_items.stem: A cart bumps a box at rest on ice. After the bump, the box moves forward at nearly constant speed. Which statement best describes the forces on the box while it is sliding?

options.A: Gravity makes the box move forward after the bump.
options.B: The box must have a forward force on it to keep moving.
options.C: Forces on the box can be balanced while it keeps moving.
options.D: Balanced forces mean the box must be at rest, so it cannot be moving.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A moving lump of clay hits a block and sticks to it. After the collision, the clay and block are no longer moving. Where did the clay's kinetic energy go?

options.A: It vanished when the objects stopped moving.
options.B: It disappeared because the clay changed shape permanently.
options.C: It stayed as kinetic energy in the clay and block even though they are not moving.
options.D: It was transferred to the clay, block, and surroundings, such as warming up and sound during the collision.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A cart is pulled on a low-friction track. The spring scale reading stays the same, and the cart speeds up. What does this show?

options.A: A force is only needed to start the cart, not to speed it up.
options.B: The pull force can stay the same while the cart's motion changes.
options.C: The pull force must be increasing because the cart is speeding up.
options.D: The pull force must be decreasing because the cart is getting faster.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
On a nearly frictionless track, a moving cart hits a cart at rest and they stick together. Which statement about the two-cart system is best?

options.A: Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved because the carts stick.
options.B: Total kinetic energy is conserved, but momentum is not because the carts stick.
options.C: Both the total momentum and the total kinetic energy of the system are conserved.
options.D: The total momentum of the system is conserved, but the total kinetic energy of the system decreases.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
